text: Elevated erythropoietin (EPO) production in hypoxia is the key factor driving enhanced hematological variables from altitude training.
The magnitude of EPO increase and acceleration of erythropoiesis depend on both the duration of hypoxic exposure and the degree of hypoxia.
Factors affecting hematological response to altitude training include hypoxic dose, training content, training background of athletes, and individual variability of EPO production.

The effectiveness of altitude training in improving hematological variables (such as red blood cell mass and hemoglobin) is inconsistent across studies, with results showing either increases or no change.
Individual variability in EPO production is a significant factor explaining why some athletes respond to altitude training while others do not.
An athlete's prior training background influences their hematological response to altitude training, meaning well-trained athletes may respond differently than less-trained athletes.
